pi.dev domain graciously donated by


exe.dev

> New issues and PRs from new contributors are auto-closed by default. Maintainers review auto-closed issues daily. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md). --- Pi is a minimal terminal coding harness. Adapt pi to your workflows, not the other way around, without having to fork and modify pi internals. Extend it with TypeScript [Extensions](#extensions), [Skills](#skills), [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates), and [Themes](#themes). Put your extensions, skills, prompt templates, and themes in [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) and share them with others via npm or git. Pi ships with powerful defaults but skips features like sub agents and plan mode. Instead, you can ask pi to build what you want or install a third party pi package that matches your workflow. Pi runs in four modes: interactive, print or JSON, RPC for process integration, and an SDK for embedding in your own apps. See [openclaw/openclaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw) for a real-world SDK integration. ## Share your OSS coding agent sessions If you use pi for open source work, please share your coding agent sessions. Public OSS session data helps improve models, prompts, tools, and evaluations using real development workflows. For the full explanation, see [this post on X](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2037811643774652911). To publish sessions, use [`badlogic/pi-share-hf`](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-share-hf). Read its README.md for setup instructions. All you need is a Hugging Face account, the Hugging Face CLI, and `pi-share-hf`. You can also watch [this video](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2041151967695634619), where I show how I publish my `pi-mono` sessions. I regularly publish my own `pi-mono` work sessions here: - [badlogicgames/pi-mono on Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/badlogicgames/pi-mono) ## Table of Contents - [Quick Start](#quick-start) - [Providers & Models](#providers--models) - [Interactive Mode](#interactive-mode) - [Editor](#editor) - [Commands](#commands) - [Keyboard Shortcuts](#keyboard-shortcuts) - [Message Queue](#message-queue) - [Sessions](#sessions) - [Branching](#branching) - [Compaction](#compaction) - [Settings](#settings) - [Context Files](#context-files) - [Customization](#customization) - [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates) - [Skills](#skills) - [Extensions](#extensions) - [Themes](#themes) - [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) - [Programmatic Usage](#programmatic-usage) - [Philosophy](#philosophy) - [CLI Reference](#cli-reference) --- ## Quick Start ```bash npm install -g --ignore-scripts @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent ``` `--ignore-scripts` disables dependency lifecycle scripts during install. Pi does not require install scripts for normal npm installs. Installer alternative: ```bash curl -fsSL https://pi.dev/install.sh | sh ``` Authenticate with an API key: ```bash export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... pi ``` Or use your existing subscription: ```bash pi /login # Then select provider ``` Then just talk to pi. By default, pi gives the model four tools: `read`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash`. The model uses these to fulfill your requests. Add capabilities via [skills](#skills), [prompt templates](#prompt-templates), [extensions](#extensions), or [pi packages](#pi-packages). **Platform notes:** [Windows](docs/windows.md) | [Termux (Android)](docs/termux.md) | [tmux](docs/tmux.md) | [Terminal setup](docs/terminal-setup.md) | [Shell aliases](docs/shell-aliases.md) --- [587 more lines in file. content.text:
containerization.md:17: Extensions run wherever the `pi` process runs. If you run host `pi` with a tool-routing extension, other custom extension tools still run on the host unless they also delegate their operations.
rpc.md:1001: - `pasteToEditor()` delegates to `setEditorText()` (no paste/collapse handling)
extensions.md:589: In parallel tool mode:
extensions.md:700: In the default parallel tool execution mode, sibling tool calls from the same assistant message are preflighted sequentially, then executed concurrently. `tool_call` is not guaranteed to see sibling tool results from that same assistant message in `ctx.sessionManager`.
extensions.md:761: In parallel tool mode, `tool_result` and `tool_execution_end` may interleave in tool completion order, while final `toolResult` message events are still emitted later in assistant source order.
extensions.md:905: For `tool_call`, this state is synchronized through the current assistant message before handlers run. In parallel tool execution mode it is still not guaranteed to include sibling tool results from the same assistant message.
extensions.md:1719: If your custom tool mutates files, use `withFileMutationQueue()` so it participates in the same per-file queue as built-in `edit` and `write`. This matters because tool calls run in parallel by default. Without the queue, two tools can read the same old file contents, compute different updates, and then whichever write lands last overwrites the other.

extensions.md:2340: - delegate `applyCompletion(...)` unless you need custom insertion behavior

They have the Pi orchestration system set up (with the `delegate`, `parallel`, `chain` tools), and when they use `parallel` mode, they don't get visibility into all 3 subagents simultaneously - they seem to only see one at a time.

Looking at the code, the issue is about how TUI rendering works for parallel subagents. The `parallel` mode spawns all subagents concurrently with a concurrency limit of 4. The TUI shows streaming updates via `emitParallelUpdate()`, but in the collapsed view (which is the default), it might only show limited info.

But the user specifically says "I don't have visibility of all 3 subagents when they are being called parallely ? Ideally I want them called sequentially" — so they want `chain` mode instead of `parallel`.

Wait, re-reading the user's message: they're using an orchestrator-mode system prompt that includes `parallel` and `chain` tools. The user says they can't see all 3 subagents when using parallel mode, and they want them sequential instead.

So the advice is: use `chain` instead of `parallel` when you want sequential execution and full visibility into each step's output.

But actually, let me also check if this is about the system prompt itself — the user has a system prompt that says to use `parallel` for independent tasks. Let me look at their system prompt context more carefully.

Looking at the system prompt in the project context, it says:
- `parallel(tasks)` — multiple specialists simultaneously
- `chain(steps)` — sequential with {previous} placeholder

And the orchestrator mode says "ALWAYS DELEGATE" with functions like `delegate(specialist, task)`, `parallel(tasks)`, `chain(steps)`.

So the user is saying that when they use `parallel`, they can't see all 3 subagents' activity in the TUI. They want sequential (chain) instead.

The issue is that in the `parallel` mode, all agents run concurrently, and the TUI updates show a combined view that might not clearly show what each agent is doing in real-time. In `chain` mode, each step runs sequentially, so the user sees one agent at a time with full visibility.

So the user's understanding is correct:
- `parallel` → all run concurrently, TUI shows a merged view, hard to track individual agents
- `chain` → runs sequentially, each step is clearly visible

The user wants to understand why this happens and/or get it changed to sequential behavior.

Let me also check if there's a way to make parallel more visible or if the user is asking about modifying the extension.

Actually, I think the user is asking a question about the pi orchestration behavior — why they can't see all 3 subagents when using parallel mode, and whether they should use sequential (chain) instead.

Let me answer based on the code analysis.

content.text:
Looking at the subagent extension code, here's the deal:

**Parallel mode** runs all tasks concurrently (up to 4 at once via `mapWithConcurrencyLimit`). The TUI renders a *single collapsed view* that updates as results stream in — but since all agents run simultaneously, the output interleaves and the collapsed view only shows the last-streamed update at any given moment. You effectively only see one agent's progress at a time.

**Chain mode** runs them sequentially — each step completes fully before the next starts. The TUI shows each step with full tool calls + output, one by one.

```
parallel → concurrent, merged TUI updates, limited per-agent visibility
chain    → sequential, full per-step visibility in TUI
```

**Two options for you:**

1. **Use `chain`** (sequential) — you already have the tool. Pass `{steps: [...]}` instead of `{tasks: [...]}`. Each agent runs to completion before the next starts. Full visibility per step.

2. **Modify the parallel renderer** — if you want parallel execution with better visibility, the extension's `renderResult` for parallel mode could be changed to show a per-agent progress panel instead of the merged collapsed view. The data's all there in `details.results` (each `SingleResult` has full `messages[]`).

Your assessment is correct — if you need to see what each subagent is doing in real-time, `chain` is the right mode. `parallel` trades visibility for speed.
